texts.orig: A B S T R A C T
texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Brine, in the form of seawater, salt lakes and geothermal water, holds massive amounts of valuable minerals. Among the variety of metallic and non-metallic elements that are recovered from brine at varying scales, Lithium (Li) has received immense interest in recent years due to its exponentially rising demand. Concentrated brine, which is the by-product of desalination, holds an even higher concentration of valuable minerals in comparison to other brine sources -thereby making it a 'resource ' for Li extraction. Tremendous progress has been made in recent years in the development of novel technologies to concentrate brine faster and efficiently to cope up with the rising production needs for Li. This review aims to highlight the recent developments in this field starting from the significance of extracting Li from brine followed by discussing recent advancements in the various nonevaporative technologies proposed in the literature. Finally, major challenges associated with these technologies in terms of sustainability, cost and technology are also presented. It can be concluded that rather than standalone  technologies,  research  must  be  focused  on  hybrid  technologies  for  efficient  utilization  of  desalination brines to improve the overall efficiency of the process. Moreover, comprehensive techno- and socio-economic analyses are essential to make these novel technologies commercially viable.

texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.1. The significance of mineral recovery from brine
texts.text: 1.1. The significance of mineral recovery from brine

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The rising global population, increasing demand for resources and deteriorating environmental quality require urgent attention to explore and  utilize  alternative  means  for  energy  and  materials  production. Whereas a great deal of research and development has been made to generate  energy  from  renewable  sources  in  a  more  sustainable  way, materials production still primarily relies on the conventional mining process which involves energy extensive methods to extract minerals from  the  existing  underground  geological  reserves.  Our  planet  is composed of 71% water of which more than 90% is highly saline, also termed  as  'brine ' .  This  brine,  which  is  distributed  in  the  form  of 'seawater ' , 'salt lakes ' and 'underground water ' , encompasses plenty of valuable  minerals  in  immense  quantities.  The  concentration  of  some valuable minerals like Li, Ni, Au, U, Sr and Rb in the oceans is several thousand times higher than the ground reserves which further signifies the  need  to  develop  economical  methods  for  their  efficient  recovery from brine [1]. Primarily, there are two major driving forces behind the research and development for sustainable minerals recovery from brine. Firstly, the rapid industrial growth and the rising demand for raw materials call for accelerated minerals exploration and extraction to cope up with the production needs. Careful analysis by several international organizations has predicted an exponential demand of some vital elements  and  hence  an  increasing  price  in  the  coming  decades.  For instance, the international energy agency (IEA) has reported that the consumption of Li, Ni and graphite by the clean energy sector alone will be quadrupled by 2040 [2]. Similarly, the global demand for Cu is expected to increase by nearly 3 million tons within the next 5 years as predicted by the recent international survey [3]. Such a sharp rise in demand can be fulfilled satisfactorily and sustainably only by exploring alternative means of resource recovery. The second major reason for an increased  interest  in  harvesting  minerals  from  brine  is  the  environmental threat posed to humans and other species as a consequence of conventional mining practices. For instance, a survey conducted in 2017 concluded that nearly 2 billion pounds of toxic waste was dumped into the waterways by mining companies posing a serious threat to humans and aquatic life [4]. Therefore, it is evident that conventional land-based mining must be minimized over time for sustaining a cleaner environment in the long run. As stated earlier, the massive volumes of brine reserves  all  over  the  globe  are  an  attractive  resource  for  recovering valuable  minerals that  can  potentially  fulfill  the  rising  industrial  demand while minimizing environmental deterioration. The brine in the form  of  salt  lakes  and  the  geothermal  brine  are  currently  the  major sources for extracting a variety of minerals due to their higher concentration. The  well-established hydrometallurgical techniques allow feasible extraction of various valuable elements such as Si, Zn, Li, Mn and various rare earth metals from these natural brine reserves. Several studies  conducted  worldwide  have  demonstrated  the  great  economic potential of recovering minerals from geothermal brines [5,6]. However, these naturally occurring brines are a dilute solution of these minerals requiring a mandatory 'concentration ' step before it can be utilized further for minerals extraction. Although the concentration of minerals in the geothermal brine can be of the order of 10 3 ppm, the seawater, which accounts for more than 90% of the naturally occurring brine, holds the most valuable minerals at a much lower concentration which is in  the  range  of  10 1 -10 2 ppm  [7].  This  makes  the  seawater  brine economically infeasible for extracting most of the minerals. Fortunately, the large-scale production of clean water via desalination, particularly reverse osmosis (RO), which uses seawater brine as the process input, results in the massive volumes of highly concentrated brine as waste which eventually holds a high concentration of these valuable minerals. The desalination brine usually gets concentrated five times as compared to the input seawater brine [8]. It is estimated that approximately 142 million m 3 of waste brine is generated every day from the desalination plants operating worldwide [9]. Therefore, in addition to the groundwater/geothermal brine, there has been an increasing interest and investments worldwide to utilize the desalination brine as a resource to harvest some valuable minerals which can positively contribute to the economy as well as the environment.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.2. Economic value
texts.text: 1.2. Economic value

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Overlooking  the  geopolitical  and  socioeconomic  factors,  that  are region-specific  and  strongly  govern  the  relative  economic  value  of extracting the particular minerals either from the land reserves or brine, the economic viability of element recovery from brine is governed by three factors: 1) the element concentration in brine, 2) its commercial consumption and 3) the market price of that particular element [1]. In general, the higher the value of these three factors, the greater is the economic viability of recovering that element from brine. Kumar et al. [10] have recently discussed the interplay of these competing factors and  determined  the  suitability  of  extracting  different  elements  from seawater  brine.  For  example,  despite  their  low  market  price,  it  is economically feasible to extract Na, K, Ca and Mg from brine due to their extremely  high  concentration  in  seawater  as  well  as  their  massive commercial consumption. Similarly, very high market price and industrial demand for U and Rb justify their extraction from brine despite their  low  concentration.  In  practice,  Na,  K,  Ca  and  Mg  are  being extracted from seawater brine as well as the desalination brine as hydroxide and carbonate salts in several regions around the world [11,12]. For the elements that exist in very low concentrations in brine yet having high market value and consumption, there is a strong need for developing  economic  and  efficient  'concentration ' methods  so  that  their extraction from brine can be holistically justified. Classical methods rely on brine evaporation in open ponds to maximize the element concentration for further purification; however, these methods are very slow (evaporation of ponds to the desired level can take up to 24 months) and strongly dependent upon the weather conditions that are region-specific and vary throughout the year. More advanced technologies based on precipitation, adsorption, solvent extraction and membranes are being developed at the laboratory and commercial scale for brine concentration  that  can  potentially  increase  the  economic  viability  of  minerals extraction from seawater and other geothermal brines.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.3. Uniqueness of Li
texts.text: 1.3. Uniqueness of Li

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Among several elements that are economically viable to be extracted from  brine,  Li  holds  paramount  importance  due  to  several  factors. Firstly, the amount of Li in the naturally occurring brine is estimated to be 230 Gt which is nearly 10 4 times higher than the land-based reserves [13]. As far as seawater brine is concerned, the concentration of Li is quite low (~0.17 ppm). However, the sub-surface and geothermal brines carry Li in concentrations as high as 10 2 -10 3 ppm which makes them an attractive resource for Li recovery. Secondly, the commercial demand for Li is expected to grow exponentially reaching up to 4.5 million tons per year by 2100 [14]. It is evident that the electronic industry will continue to be the main consumer of Li in addition to the automobile industry where the focus has been shifted to mass production of electric vehicles (EV). The consumption of Li in the production of heavy-duty batteries  for  powering  EV  is  several  orders  of  magnitude  higher  as compared to the batteries used for portable electronics. Because of this rapid demand, the price of Li metal has increased by approximately 10 folds during the last 20 years [15]. Currently, Li carbonate and Li hydroxide are the two primary minerals that are used at the commercial scale for Li extraction. During the year 2020 -2021, the demand for these commodities rose by nearly 30% while the prices rose remarkably by about 300% [16]. Therefore, it is evident that both the demand and price of Li will continue to soar and a greater focus is required to efficiently extract Li from the existing brine reserves. texts.orig: Recent years witnessed accelerated research related to Li in general and more specifically on recovering Li from brine sources. Fig. 1 shows the increased interest in Li-related studies over the last 10 years based on the search results collected from the Scopus database. While there are some recent reviews covering recent advancements on the Li recovery from brine sources [17 -21], most of them were limited to a specific technology  with  an  exception  to  the  work  published  in  2019  [22]. Meanwhile, this review aims to cover an overview of recent advancements in the non-evaporative technologies in general for Li recovery with separate sections covering different technologies and their hybrid methods.  More  importantly,  this  review  puts  forward  the  idea  of recovering valuable Li from desalination brines which is indeed a 'from waste to wealth ' concept.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Brine concentration methods for Li recovery
texts.text: 2. Brine concentration methods for Li recovery

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  a  conventional  process,  Li-containing  brine  obtained  from  the source is initially concentrated to an appropriate concentration (around 6000 ppm Li) for viable recovery [23,24]. This is often achieved by the evaporation  of  raw  brine  in  large  open-air  shallow  ponds  via  solar irradiation and wind. Fig. 2 shows the schematics of the conventional evaporation process. Depending upon the nature of brine and environmental conditions, the evaporation can be carried out in multiple steps while harvesting salts of other elements such as Na, Mg and K [25]. The final brine, which is rich in Li chloride, is subsequently pumped to the treatment plants where further chemical reactions are carried out to obtain commercial-grade Li carbonate which then serves as the main raw material to produce desired Li compounds or Li metal. As discussed previously, this evaporative method is time-intensive and not applicable at all geographical locations due to significant variations in climate. In addition, the efficiency of this method is heavily dependent upon the brine composition which varies greatly from one location to another. The presence of other ions in a higher concentration may cause their coprecipitation that will further complicate the Li recovery process. For instance, Mg 2 + ions possess very similar chemistry to that of Li + ions and they can coprecipitate as Mg carbonate along with Li carbonate which greatly complicates further recovery of the desired Li salts [26]. texts.text: In  a  conventional  process,  Li-containing  brine  obtained  from  the source is initially concentrated to an appropriate concentration (around 6000 ppm Li) for viable recovery [23,24]. This is often achieved by the evaporation  of  raw  brine  in  large  open-air  shallow  ponds  via  solar irradiation and wind. Fig. 2 shows the schematics of the conventional evaporation process. Depending upon the nature of brine and environmental conditions, the evaporation can be carried out in multiple steps while harvesting salts of other elements such as Na, Mg and K [25]. The final brine, which is rich in Li chloride, is subsequently pumped to the treatment plants where further chemical reactions are carried out to obtain commercial-grade Li carbonate which then serves as the main raw material to produce desired Li compounds or Li metal. As discussed previously, this evaporative method is time-intensive and not applicable at all geographical locations due to significant variations in climate. In addition, the efficiency of this method is heavily dependent upon the brine composition which varies greatly from one location to another. The presence of other ions in a higher concentration may cause their coprecipitation that will further complicate the Li recovery process. For instance, Mg 2 + ions possess very similar chemistry to that of Li + ions and they can coprecipitate as Mg carbonate along with Li carbonate which greatly complicates further recovery of the desired Li salts [26]. texts.orig: 2.1. Precipitation
texts.text: 2.1. Precipitation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Precipitation-based methods are relatively simple and commercially favorable because of their low cost. The earliest successful experiments to precipitate Li as Li aluminate from dead sea brine were reported by several researchers using aluminum chloride under optimal conditions of pH and temperature [28 -30]. Precipitation of Li as Li carbonate in brine was also reported using a multi-step precipitation process [31]. The  main  issue  in  Li  precipitation  is  the  co-precipitation  of  Mg  and therefore the researchers have proposed different precipitation methods depending upon the relative Mg/Li ratio in brine.
texts.text: Precipitation-based methods are relatively simple and commercially favorable because of their low cost. The earliest successful experiments to precipitate Li as Li aluminate from dead sea brine were reported by several researchers using aluminum chloride under optimal conditions of pH and temperature [28 -30]. Precipitation of Li as Li carbonate in brine was also reported using a multi-step precipitation process [31]. The  main  issue  in  Li  precipitation  is  the  co-precipitation  of  Mg  and therefore the researchers have proposed different precipitation methods depending upon the relative Mg/Li ratio in brine.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the case of brines with a low Mg/Li ratio (less than 6) [32], 'carbonate precipitation ' is common in which Mg is first precipitated via calcium oxide followed by the precipitation of Li as Li carbonate which is achieved  by  adding  sodium  carbonate  [33].  In  some  brine  reserves, where crude Li carbonate exists with low Mg/Li ratios, 'carbonization precipitation ' , which involves purging carbon dioxide in brine, is used to precipitate Li as lithium bicarbonate [34]. A combination of both carbonate  and  carbonization  precipitation  has  also  been  employed  to obtain high purity Li carbonate [35].
texts.text: In the case of brines with a low Mg/Li ratio (less than 6) [32], 'carbonate precipitation ' is common in which Mg is first precipitated via calcium oxide followed by the precipitation of Li as Li carbonate which is achieved  by  adding  sodium  carbonate  [33].  In  some  brine  reserves, where crude Li carbonate exists with low Mg/Li ratios, 'carbonization precipitation ' , which involves purging carbon dioxide in brine, is used to precipitate Li as lithium bicarbonate [34]. A combination of both carbonate  and  carbonization  precipitation  has  also  been  employed  to obtain high purity Li carbonate [35].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the case of a high Mg/Li ratio, which is usually the case with most of the naturally occurring brine resources, 'aluminate precipitation ' and 'Mg precipitation ' methods are employed for Li recovery. In aluminate precipitation, aluminum chloride is added along with sodium hydroxide to generate aluminum hydroxide which then selectively precipitates Li as  Li  aluminate  [28].  This  method,  which  is  environmentally  more sustainable, has shown excellent recovery of Li from brines with high Mg/Li  ratios  [36,37].  In  recent  years,  some  advanced  and  activated materials have been developed to extract Li from brine with higher efficiency. These include Al-Ca alloys [38], Al-NaCl mixture [39] which provided  Li  recovery  as  high  as  94%  from  high  Mg/Li  ratio  brines. Engineered  double  hydroxides,  such  as  Li  aluminum  layered  double hydroxide chloride, have also been developed for recovering more than 90% of Li in a low-cost and environmentally friendly manner [40]. Mg precipitation is another approach to deal with a high Mg/Li ratio as it allows reducing the Mg/Li ratio for convenient Li recovery. Ammonium oxalate and sodium carbonate were found to be effective in precipitating 98% of Mg under optimal reaction conditions [41]. Oxalic acid was also found to be effective in precipitating more than 95% of Mg as Mg oxalate in brine with Mg/Li ratio as high as 21 [42]. Researchers have shown that integrated and multi-step methods are effective for precipitating and separating 99% of Mg from brines with a high Mg/Li ratio. An et al. [31] utilized calcium hydroxide and sodium oxalate separately for twostage precipitation that allowed removal of Mg along with some other unwanted impurities like boron and sulfur. The process flow is shown in Fig. 3a. Similarly, Wang et al. [43] proposed a method involving several steps and chemicals for selective Mg and Li recovery as shown in Fig. 3b. Although  the  process  was  extensive,  it  provided  almost  complete removal of Mg along with high yield (91%) and purity (99.7%) of Li carbonate.

Despite  being  one  of  the  oldest methods to recover Li from brine sources, precipitation is still popular due to its easy scale-up and low cost. Nevertheless, significant efforts should  be  made  to  improve  energy  efficiency  and  to  eliminate  the environmental impact associated with the addition of chemical reagents.

texts.orig: 2.2. Ion-exchange and sorption
texts.text: 2.2. Ion-exchange and sorption

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From the cost and efficiency perspective, ion-exchange methods are gaining increasing interest as the 'Li sieves ' show excellent selectivity and adsorption towards Li ions. Moreover, this method is effective even for low Li concentrations which is usually the case with most naturally occurring brines [52]. This method involves the systematic design and fabrication  of  mesoporous materials with  active sites that are  highly selective towards Li ions [53].
texts.text: From the cost and efficiency perspective, ion-exchange methods are gaining increasing interest as the 'Li sieves ' show excellent selectivity and adsorption towards Li ions. Moreover, this method is effective even for low Li concentrations which is usually the case with most naturally occurring brines [52]. This method involves the systematic design and fabrication  of  mesoporous materials with  active sites that are  highly selective towards Li ions [53].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Manganese  and  titanium-based  adsorbents  have  been  reported extensively in recent years for their ability to selectively separate Li from brine sources. Particularly, delithiated manganese and titanium-based adsorbents formed by acid treatment of Li-containing oxides are studied widely in recent years [54 -60]. The pioneering work in the design of manganese-based adsorbents to selectively capture Li was carried out by Chitrakar et al. who synthesized manganese oxide-based adsorbents for elective recovery of Li from brine [61]. They utilized LiMnO2 as a precursor to fabricate delithiated oxide adsorbent with a chemical formula Li1.6Mn1.6O4.  For  3  days,  the  adsorbent  was  able  to  capture  Li  from seawater with a capacity as high as 40 mg.g 1 with a recovery of above 80% (Fig. 4a). In their further investigations [62,63], they showed that the same performance can be achieved by this adsorbent within 1 day by adding a small amount of sodium bi -carbonate and hydrochloric acid in seawater brine (Fig. 4b and c). Meanwhile, Manganese-based adsorbents suffer from poor chemical stability due to their weak metal -oxygen bond making the fabrication and regeneration process more challenging [55]. To improve the chemical stability, Fumihiko  Ohashi and Yutaka Tai presented surface-modified adsorbents by treating Li1.33Mn1.67O4  with transition  metal  oxide  oligomers  which  on  subsequent  calcination resulted in adsorbents with thin metal oxide coating [55]. As synthesized adsorbents exhibited superior chemical stability and an adsorption capacity over 50 mg.g 1 . Similarly, Mn dissolution can be also inhibited by metal doping like the one reported in [56] where doping was done with Al 3 + due to its comparable size to that of Mn 3 + and a stronger metal -oxygen bond. Metal doping not only improved the stability of adsorbents but also enhanced the performance towards Li extraction.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: On the other hand, titania-based adsorbents are superior in terms of withstanding harsh chemical conditions owing to their stronger metal -oxygen bond. Hui et al. fabricated H2TiO3 ion exchanger using either Li carbonate or Li hydroxide with titania followed by calcination at high temperatures. The H2TiO3  showed high selectivity for Li ions with an adsorption capacity of 25.34 mg.g 1 when the brine from natural gas wells was used [64]. However, conventional solid adsorbents often tend to  agglomerate  leading  to  lower  effective  surface  area  available  for adsorption. To overcome this issue, ion sieves of titanium oxide-based fibers were reported in a recent study conducted by Wei et al. [57]. A series of porous fibrous titanium-based Li ion sieves were fabricated in their study by electrospinning and subsequent calcination. Fabricating porous  adsorbents  in  fibrous  structure  enhanced  the  availability  of active sites thus exhibiting superior performance compared to a nonfibrous  structure.  Porous  structure  enhanced  the  Li  intercalation  and deintercalation providing a high adsorption capacity of 59.1 mg.g 1 at 298 K.

texts.orig: In addition to this, several nanostructured adsorbents such as those based on Li manganese oxide spinel [69,70] have also been reported with high Li uptake capacity from different brines; however, high cost and environmental concerns hinder their application at the commercial scale. Very recently, interest has increased in utilizing more advanced and  sustainable  materials  to  fabricate  efficient  adsorbents  that  can selectively recover Li from brine. Absi et al. [71] fabricated composite adsorbents from roasted date pits using copper and nickel based ferrocyanides. As compared to the pristine roasted date pits, the composites showed significantly higher adsorption capacity for Li from the seawater RO brine. The Li uptake in the case of composites was found to be above 99%, Fig. 4d, which was attributed to the high density of active sites. In another  study  [72],  the  same  group  of  researchers  impregnated  the roasted  date  pits  with  cellulose  nanocrystals  and  ionic  liquid.  As depicted in Fig. 4e, the composite provided almost complete recovery of Li  from  the  seawater  RO brine  as compared to the pristine date pits which  provided  only  50%  Li  recovery.  Composite  aerogel  based  on H2TiO3 and cellulose were recently fabricated by Qian et al. [73] who reported more than 95% Li recovery by this composite from seawater as presented in Fig. 4f. Table 2 presents a comparison of Li recovery reported in the recent studies by adsorption/ion exchange.

texts.orig: of Li chloride with good selectivity towards the unwanted salts [82]. Tributyl phosphate (TBP) is the most popular and widely investigated extractant to selectively recover Li by the solvent extraction process. For example, the extraction system composed of TBP/kerosene and FeCl3 was found to be suitable to recover Li from the brine sources with high Mg/Li ratios. In this system, TBP serves as a neutral organophosphorus extractant while kerosene acts as a diluent. FeCl3 aids in coextraction for Li  recovery  [83].  Zhou  et  al.  [84]  utilized  this  extraction  system  to recover Li from brine and one of their experimental results is shown in Fig. 5a where MgCl2  serves as the main chloride source. The extractability (E) of the iron increased with the concentration of the chloride ion, while the partition coefficient (D) of Li increased with the extractability of iron and chloride concentration. Based on these results, it was concluded that  Fe  extraction  is  the  precondition  for  Li  extraction.  A synergistic system comprised of tributyl phosphate (TBP) and dioctyl phthalate (DOP) or acetyl tributyl citrate (ATBC) or tri-n-butyl citrate (TBC) in kerosene and FeCl3  was used by researchers for extracting Li from brine [85]. As shown in Fig. 5c, the TBP/DOP system provided the highest Li extraction which reached nearly 90% at the solvent concentration of 80%.

Tributyl phosphate (TBP) is the most popular and widely investigated extractant to selectively recover Li by the solvent extraction process. For example, the extraction system composed of TBP/kerosene and FeCl3 was found to be suitable to recover Li from the brine sources with high Mg/Li ratios. In this system, TBP serves as a neutral organophosphorus extractant while kerosene acts as a diluent. FeCl3 aids in coextraction for Li  recovery  [83].  Zhou  et  al.  [84]  utilized  this  extraction  system  to recover Li from brine and one of their experimental results is shown in Fig. 5a where MgCl2  serves as the main chloride source. The extractability (E) of the iron increased with the concentration of the chloride ion, while the partition coefficient (D) of Li increased with the extractability of iron and chloride concentration. Based on these results, it was concluded that  Fe  extraction  is  the  precondition  for  Li  extraction.  A synergistic system comprised of tributyl phosphate (TBP) and dioctyl phthalate (DOP) or acetyl tributyl citrate (ATBC) or tri-n-butyl citrate (TBC) in kerosene and FeCl3  was used by researchers for extracting Li from brine [85]. As shown in Fig. 5c, the TBP/DOP system provided the highest Li extraction which reached nearly 90% at the solvent concentration of 80%.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  systems  where  TBP  and  FeCl3 are  used  as  extractant  and  coextractant, Li + combines with [FeCl4] and subsequently forms a complex with TPB in the organic phase. This not only necessitates high acid concentrations in the stripping section to recover Li but also demands further neutralization of residual acids in the regenerated organic phase resulting  in  loss  of  some  Fe 3 + .  To  avoid,  these  complex  procedures during every extraction/stripping stage, different co-extracts have been reported in the literature. For example, Su et al. investigated Li extraction  using  TBP,  FeCl3 and  2-ethylhexyl  phosphonic  acid  mono-2ethylhexyl ester (P507) system where water could be used in the stripping stage eliminating acid treatment and thus enabling direct recycling of the organic phase [86]. In a three-stage extraction process, 99.8% of the Li in the lake brine could be recovered by Fe 3 + loaded organic phase containing 40% and 30% of TBP and P507, respectively.

[87] employed an extraction system based on thenoyltrifluoroacetone, trioctylphosphine oxide and kerosene to extract Li from seawater and concentrated seawater under different aqueous to extraction volume ratios (1.0 -10). As depicted in Fig. 5b, the extraction efficiency decreased with an increasing aqueous to extraction volume ratio and remained higher for seawater as compared to concentrated seawater. In a different approach, Shi et al. proposed the removal coions namely Ca 2 + and Mg 2 + and in the brine using di-(2-ethylhexyl) phosphoric  acid  (D2EHPA)  as  extractant  to  recover  Li  [88].  Using multistage centrifugal extractors, 99.05% and 98.48% of the Ca and Mg were removed from the solution with Li loss controlled to 5.22%.

texts.orig: Ionic liquids with low volatility, low melting point and high thermal stability,  are  considered as a promising green alternative to the conventional diluents as an extraction medium to recover Li from different kinds  of  brine.  For  instance,  Shi  et  al.  [89]  employed  a  system comprising of the ionic liquid (extraction medium), sodium perchlorate (co-extraction reagent) and TBP (extractant) to extract Li from salt lake brine.  With  an  increasing  phase  ratio,  the  Li  extraction  efficiency increased reaching above 90% as presented in Fig. 5d. In another study [90], the same group of researchers showed that an optimal content of the ionic liquid in the extraction system is important to maximize the Li extraction  efficiency  from  brine  (Fig.  5e).  Similarly,  Yang  et  al.  proposed, an extraction system composed of hydroxyl functionalized ionic liquid, 1-hydroxyethyl-3-methyl imidazolium bis(trifluoromethylsulfonyl)imide  and  a  neutral  ligand,  trialkyl  phosphine oxide, was used for Li extraction from alkaline brine [91]. As shown in Fig. 5f, they also found that an optimal proportion of the ionic liquid yields the highest Li extraction efficiency. A comparison of the various extraction  system  and  their  efficiency  in  recovering  Li  from  brine sources have been summarized in Table 3.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Utilizing nanostructured membranes, having a pore size in the range of sub-nanometer to few nanometers, has gained a lot of interest for Li recovery. This technology is promising due to its advantages of better energy  efficiency,  ease  of  operation  and  process  continuity  [102]. Although several membrane-based methods have been employed at the laboratory, pilot as well as the commercial scale for extracting Li from brine, 'nanofiltration ' (NF) and 'electrodialysis ' (ED) have been, by far, the most prevalent ones at all scales due to their moderate cost, low environmental foot-print and higher selectivity [19]. Several studies are based on employing commercially available NF membranes; however, like all other recovery methods, the high Mg/Li ratio in brine remains a challenge that limits the selective recovery of Li over Mg [103,104]. Control of the solution pH and the use of NF combined with membranedistillation (MD) has yielded slightly higher Li recovery from brine as reported by some researchers [105,106].

texts.orig: Research has shown that the use of positively charged membranes is highly beneficial for cations separations due to the Donnan exclusion effect  [107].  This  principle,  therefore,  has  also  been  successfully employed for the  efficient  recovery  of  Li  from  brine.  Li  et  al.  [108] fabricated a composite hollow-fiber membrane consisting of a positively charged polyamide (PA) layer on polyacrylonitrile (PAN) based ultrafiltration (UF) support. Due to the Donnan effect, the membrane provided  a  much  higher  rejection  of  high  valency  cations  (Mg 2 + )  as compared to the low valency cations (Li + )  as  depicted  in  Fig.  6a.  A positively  charged  membrane  with  much  better  performance  was designed and fabricated by Zhang et al. [109] who generated a positively charged NF layer on polyether sulfone UF supports via interfacial polymerization.  To  enhance  the  permeability,  functionalized  multiwalled  carbon  nanotubes  (MWCNTs)  were  also  incorporated  within the NF layer. As shown in Fig. 6b, the membrane rejected more than 95% of  Mg and Ca while the Li rejection was only 20% which supported strong  potential  of  composite  nanostructured  membranes  for  high  Li recovery. A positively charged PA based NF flat-sheet membrane was fabricated over polyetherimide cross-linked supports by Li et al. [110]. A

texts.orig: Accordingly, in recent years, several studies focused on the fabrication of polyamide membranes for recovering Li from Mg-rich sources through NF [112 -117]. Besides steric hindrance, Mg 2 + ions experience considerably  higher  electrostatic  repulsion  from  positively  charged membranes. Accordingly, most of these studies focused on enhancing the positive charge on the polyamide layer by anchoring charged species on the membrane surface. For instance, Wu et al. proposed 1-(3-aminopropyl)-3-methylimidazolium bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide ([MimAP][Tf2N]) modified polyamide membranes with a positive surface charge from the quaternary ammonium group in the grafted ionic liquid [112]. Modified membranes exhibited a pure water flux of 37.8 LMH maintaining a rejection  of  89%,  83.8%  and  24.4%  for  MgSO4, MgCl2 and LiCl, respectively, while NaCl rejection was as low as 30.1%. However, for simulated brine with a Mg/Li ratio of 20, the membrane could offer a selectivity of 8.12 bringing down the ratio in the permeate to  3.5.  It  was  also  observed  that  the  membrane  performance  significantly deteriorated while dealing with a higher Mg/Li ratio. The Mg rejection dropped significantly at higher Mg concentration in the field possibly due to higher Mg driving force across the membrane. Similarly, higher ion concentration strengthened the concentration polarization effect thereby decreasing the flux to 23.19 LMH. Similarly, dual skin layered NF  membrane  was  prepared  in  by  Yang  et  al.  where polyethylene-imine (PEI) was grafted on the polyamide layer to enhance the  positive  charge  on  the  membrane  surface  [115].  The  synergistic effect of size sieving achieved by the polyamide membrane and electrostatic repulsion from the positively charged PEI chains on the surface provided a very high rejection for MgCl2 thereby achieving a high separation factor of 33.4 under optimum conditions. Very recently, Feng and  co-workers  used  quaternized  bipyridine  to  modify  polyamide membranes  prepared  by  interracial  polymerization  of  PEI  and  TMC [117]. Modified membranes exhibited 2.8 times the flux offered by the pristine membranes which was attributed to the structural rearrangement and partial degradation of the polyamide layer resulting in a loose selective layer compared to pristine membranes. However, Mg/Li ratio in the feed could be brought down approximately to 8 from 50 due to the enhanced surface charge.

texts.orig: While the NF membranes discussed above were mostly positively charged, a recent investigation conducted by Wu et al. demonstrated that Li/Mg separation could be achieved by using negatively charged membranes [118]. In  contrast  to  the  positively  charged  membranes, these membranes exhibited higher retention towards Li in comparison to Mg due to their higher surface charge in LiCl solution. Surface modification was carried out by anchoring BTESE through dip coating on TiO2 ceramic  membranes  followed  by  calcination  at  400 ◦ C,  thereby achieving a narrow pore size distribution of 0.63 -1.66 nm. As prepared membranes exhibited a high permeability of 9.5 LMH per bar for salt solution  with  74.7%  and  20.3%  rejection  towards  LiCl  and  MgCl2, respectively.  Considering  the  excellent  chemical  stability  of  ceramic membranes in comparison to polymeric counterparts, the observed results  are  promising  for  Li  recovery.  Nevertheless,  further  systematic investigations are necessary to evaluate the performance of the membrane in mixed solvents and as well as the real salt lake brine.

texts.orig: A combined membrane-based NF-distillation process has been proposed recently  to  recover  and  enrich  Li  from  its  sources.  NF  can  be employed as a pre-treatment before MD thereby enabling the removal of divalent  ions  (Ca 2 + ,  Mg 2 + )  that  are  prone  to  form  crystals  on  the distillation membrane. For example, Pramanik et al. demonstrated Li enrichment from simulated brine with commercial membranes using NF followed by MD [106]. Mg/Li ratio in the brine is brought down to 0.19 from 10 by the NF membranes operating at a transmembrane pressure of 8 bar which was subsequently enriched up to 80% using MD. Similar technology was proposed by Park et al. who reported a concentration of Li  12-fold  using  combined  NF-MD  process  just  by  using  commercial membranes [119]. While such systems are more sustainable, faster and cheaper in terms of capital cost, the operational cost is still expected to be  higher  than  conventional  Li  enrichment  units  due  to  the  thermal

texts.orig: More  recently,  'electrodialysis  (ED) ' has  been  gaining  increasing popularity to recover Li from different brines. In ED, the cation and anion exchange membranes are placed alternatively while an electrical field is applied across this membrane stack. As a result, the cations and anions pass through the respective ion exchange membrane and eventually towards their respective electrode [120]. For selective Li recovery, monovalent ion-exchange membranes, which separate monovalent ions from divalent ions, have been proposed and employed for ED. Nie et al. [121] reported Li recovery of more than 95% from synthetic brine having Mg/Li ratio as high as 150 while employing monovalent cation exchange  membrane  in  ED.  As  presented  in  Fig.  6d,  the  Mg/Li  was reduced by up to 30 times with 1 h of operation. These results were remarkable as previously no other study reported such a drastic reduction in Mg/Li ratio within such a short period. In another study by Chen et al. [122] who also used a monovalent cation exchange membrane for recovering Li via ED, it was found that the Li recovery rate is strongly dependent upon the initial Mg/Li ratio in brine. The lower Mg/Li ratio favored faster Li recovery and vice versa as shown in Fig. 6e. Bipolar membrane electrodialysis (BMED), which utilizes bipolar membrane, is another proposed approach for extracting Li hydroxide from brine. This technology offers higher current efficiency and good control over the energy consumption (Fig. 6f) while precipitating Li hydroxide from Li carbonate solution, as demonstrated by Jiang et al. [123]. Moreover, the obtained product purity was reported to be above 98% which is desirable  for  the  post-production  of  various  Li-based  products.  Other  researchers  [124]  have  also  reported  good  control  over  the  process efficiency and energy consumption by varying different feed parameters in  BMED  while  recovering  Li  from  brine. texts.orig: 2.5. Electrochemical methods
texts.text: 2.5. Electrochemical methods

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Various novel electrochemical methods of Li recovery such as battery type, electrochemically switched ion exchange and electrodialysis have been introduced in recent years [20]. While studies related to electrodialysis have been previously discussed under membrane-based technologies, this section will briefly review recent reports related to the rest. In a battery-type electrochemical process, electrodes capable of ion extraction have been employed for selectively capturing Li from the feed and releasing it into the recovery solution [131]. Electrodes developed should have high selectivity towards Li, high Li capacity along with low energy consumption and stability for long-term operation. The electrochemically switched ion exchange process is an efficient and environmentally friendly process combining electrochemistry with ionexchange thereby  enabling  Li  recovery  even  from  low  concentration brines [131]. One of the electrodes in this process must have the ability to rapidly absorb and release desired ions from the system by adjusting the  electrochemical  potential.  Besides  this,  capacitive  deionization (CDI) has drawn immense interest especially in combination with ion exchange  membranes  which  is  briefly  discussed  in  Section  2.6.  CDI process involves selective adsorption of anions and cations at the porous electrodes with the aid of an electric field [132,133].

texts.orig: 1990s  as  a  cathodic  material  for  Li-ion  batteries  [134],  has  been  of particular interest for capturing Li from brine. The pioneering work to utilize LFP to recover Li from brine was conducted by Pasta et al. [135]. They showed selective recovery of Li from brine by utilizing carboncoated LFP as a Li intercalation media. Under optimal conditions, they reported Li/Na ratio of 5 in the recovery solution as compared to 0.01 in the initial brine. Recently, Sun et al. [136] utilized LFP and iron phosphate as cathode and anode, respectively, to recover more than 90% of Li from the geothermal brine. The Li adsorption capacity of the electrode increased  with  an  increasing  operating  voltage  as  shown  in  Fig.  7a. Trocoli et al. [137] utilized λ -MnO2 as Li-capturing electrode and nickel hexacyanoferrate as Li-exclusion electrode to recover Li from salt lake brine. In addition to achieving high Li yield and purity, the stability of λ -MnO2 based electrode was found to be much higher as compared to LFP as shown via cyclic performance in Fig. 7b. Kim et al. [138] proposed the use of zinc as a negative electrode while using LiMn2O4 as a positive  electrode  to  recover  Li  from  salt  lake  brine.  As  depicted  in Fig. 7c, Li was selectively and efficiently recovered during successive electrochemical cycles. Also, the reported energy consumption was 6.3 Wh/mol of Li recovered which was significantly low as compared to previous systems.

texts.orig: Recovering Li from high Mg brines through electrochemical methods has been addressed in some recent studies. For example, LiMn2O4  and Li1-xMn2O4  were  employed  as  electrode  materials  in  a  recent  study which was demonstrated to be effective for brines with Mg/Li ratio as high as 147.8. The system provided a rejection rate of impurities as high as 97% with a Li recovery of around 83.3% with a stable Li intercalation capacity (Fig. 7d). Similarly, Mu et al. [139] coated 3D graphite felts with λ -MnO2 and LiMn2O4 to fabricate cathode and anode, respectively, for  recovering Li  from a simulated brine containing 100 mg/L of Li. LiMn2O4 electrode material was fabricated with a mesoporous structure templating with mesoporous silica. As synthesized electrode materials offered a high surface area with active sites enabling enhanced intercalation and deintercalation LiMn2O4  phases. Accordingly, their electrochemical system provided a high extraction efficiency of 26.14 mg.

texts.orig: In  another  recent  work  [140],  an  electrochemical  system  was demonstrated based on the reversible redox reaction of ferri-/ferrocyanide while using λ -MnO2 as an adsorbent electrode. As shown in Fig. 7f, a very high uptake and selectivity of Li as compared to other cations were achieved by this system. Due to the high affinity of λ -MnO2  towards Li, this system offered surprisingly high selectivity of 804 and 387, respectively for Li over Mg and Na for experiments with real salt lake brine. While most of the electrochemical systems explained here are limited by energy consumption to a great extent, in a recent study, J. Niu et al. proposed an electrically switched ion exchange system for efficient separation of Li salt from lake brines [141]. λ -MnO2  and BiO-Cl@PPy nanocomposites  were  chosen  as  the  electroactive  materials  considering the high Li + selectivity of the λ -MnO2  and Cl selectivity of the modified PPy layer. PPy layer was doped with Cl during polymerization to render excellent Cl selectivity. The proposed system not only offered excellent Li + but also consumed very low energy of 1.007 Wh/ mol as energy consumption for electrode regeneration was compensated by the electrical energy produced during LiCl uptake. Additionally, the system could maintain 96% of the LiCl uptake even up to 100 cycles making  this  technology,  with  self-electrical-energy  recuperation,  a promising  method  for  Li  recovery  from  brines.  Various  electroactive materials used in recent investigations and their corresponding extraction efficiencies and energy consumption in extracting Li is summarized in Table 5.

texts.orig: Besides  the  above  specific  methods  for  Li  recovery  from  brine,  a combination of different methods has been examined by researchers to potentially  obtain  better  Li  recovery  efficiency.  In  this  regard,  the membrane technology has been very effective and plenty of studies have been  conducted  on  the  fabrication  and  performance  of  polymeric membranes incorporated with adsorbents [150] and ionic liquids [151]. Such  advanced  membranes  have  been  fabricated  and  evaluated  by several  researchers  for  recovering  Li  from  brine.  Song  et  al.  [152] employed 'liquid-liquid membrane ' extraction by fabricating  a nanoporous  ion  exchange  membrane  based  on  PES  and  sulfonated  poly (phenyl ether ketone). In parallel, tributylphosphate (TBP) was used as the  extractant  and  kerosene  as  the  diluent.  The  system  exhibited appreciable Li extraction from a high salinity brine, Fig. 8a, without any solvent  leakage  during  the  extraction  process  which  was  one  of  the limitations of the previously reported membrane-based systems. Xing et al. [153] extracted Li from salt lake brine by fabricating a new type of solvent-resistant membrane based on poly (ethyleneco -vinyl alcohol) while using TBP/kerosene as a liquid extracting system. The membrane showed long-term stability along with steady Li recovery as depicted in Fig. 8b. Zante et al. [154] impregnated porous PVDF membranes with an ionic liquid while using TBP as a carrier medium. Using this combination, they demonstrated steady Li recovery from brine containing a high concentration of Mg 2 + ions, Fig. 8c.

texts.orig: The adsorption method has also shown  good synergy with membrane-based methods for efficient Li recovery from different kinds of  brine.  A  membrane-type adsorbent was prepared by Umeno et al. [155]  by  incorporating  Li1.33Mn1.67O4  spinel  in  poly(vinyl  chloride) (PVC). More than 95% of Li was recovered from seawater brine by this membrane-type adsorbent within 10 h as shown in Fig. 8d. In another study by Chung et al. [156], MnO2 particles were incorporated as ionexchange adsorbents in polysulfone (PS) membrane to recover Li from seawater.  The  MnO2-PS  membrane  exhibited  high  selectivity  and adsorption for Li as presented in Fig. 8e. PVDF membranes incorporating Li4Mn5O12 spinel as adsorbent also exhibited good selectivity for Li over other cations as demonstrated by Sun et al. [157], Fig. 8f.

texts.orig: Besides these, several novel approaches to recover Li through hybrid systems have been reported in recent years. These include ion-imprinted membranes (IIM), Li ion sieve membranes (LISM), membrane distillation  crystallization  (MDC)  and  membrane  capacitive  deionization (MCDI) [19]. IIMs have gained significant attention due to their superior performance over conventional membrane adsorption systems [158 -162]. IIMs are prepared by grafting desired Li imprinted molecules on the membrane surface thereby enhancing ion selectivity by adsorption alongside membrane size sieving. The formation of a tailor-made Li recognization site is primarily important and challenging in developing Li selective IIMs. Among these, 12-crown-4 is particularly reported as an efficient molecules for ion imprinting on the membrane surface due to its coordination with Li + ions via macrocyclic effect [161 -163]. Lu et al. prepared Li imprinted membrane on PES substrates with a multilayer structure comprising of silica nanoparticles and Li anchored 12-crown-4 polymer. Membrane exhibited a permselectivity of 7.39 and 9.86 for Na + and K + over Li + ions and could maintain a binding capacity around 88.1% even after 5 cycles of operation. Despite their superior performance, the manufacturing cost and the fouling resistance are the major concern  when  it  comes  to  scaling  up  of  IIMs.  Similarly  LISM  which combines  the  benefits  of  ion  sieves  and  membranes,  eliminates  the challenges associated with powdery adsorbents while holding a strong potential  for  continous  operation  [18].  A  group  of  researchers  has demonstrated that nanofibrous membranes incorporating Li-based adsorbents can be highly effective for capturing Li from seawater. The idea is to make use of the high surface area offered by nanofibers which is not achievable with conventional membranes. In their initial work [164], they fabricated nanofibrous membranes by electrospinning a solution composed of Li1.6Mn1.6O4  (HMO) and poly(acrylonitrile)  (PAN).  The resulting composite membrane with a significantly higher surface area showed the highest selectivity towards Li, as shown in Fig. 8g, when the seawater  desalination  brine  was  used  as  feed.  In  a  subsequent  work [165], Li1.6Mn1.6O4  was mixed with PS solution to fabricate a similar electrospun membrane which showed much higher selectivity towards Li  as  compared  to  the  other  cations,  Fig.  8h.  The  same  group  of  researchers, in their recent work [166], investigated Li1.6Mn1.6O4/PAN nanofibrous membranes under a wide range of operating conditions and showed a high separation factor for Li under varying conditions when seawater brine was used as feed, Fig. 8i.

texts.orig: Membrane capacitive deionization (MCDI) is another emerging ecofriendly and hybrid approach for Li recovery which utilizes ion exchange membranes in a conventional capacitive deionization process [167,168]. By employing monovalent cation exchange membranes selective to Li between the electrodes, selective recovery of Li ions could be achieved from a mixed ion solution. For example, Shi et al. reported Li extraction by employing Li selective cation exchange membranes with a selectivity of 2.95 over magnesium from LiCl/MgCl2  solution [168]. With the application of voltage, Li ions migrate through the cation exchange membranes and get accumulated on the cathode surface while Mg ions are blocked and retained in the feed solution. It is worth noting that  the  energy  consumption  was  just  0.0018  kWh  mol 1 which  is significantly lower than many reported studies. However, MCDI based Li recovery  systems  are  at  their  early  stage  requiring  further  extensive research before being implemented at a larger scale. On the other hand, membrane distillation  crystallization  (MDC)  couples  distillation  with crystallization to improve the crystallization rate and provides controlled  nucleation  compared  to  the  conventional  crystallization processes [169]. In this process, brine is initially enriched by removing water  using  membrane  distillation  followed  by  Li  recovery  through crystallization,  thereby  potentially  achieving  a  zero  liquid  discharge technology.  However,  scale-up  MDC  is  very  challenging  due  to  the requirement of additional cleaning/separation to isolate Li from residual impurities or co-ions in the concentrate. Table 6 provides a comparison of Li adsorption capacity reported in recent years through the hybrid methods.

Almost all the published research has overlooked this issue and a general understanding would be to dispose this brine back to the underground aquifers and nearby salt lakes [174]. However, this practice would drastically alter the composition of the naturally occurring brine reserves leading to further complications of the subsequent recovery cycles. In addition, threats posed to the aquatic ecosystem, especially in lakes, due to the significantly altered composition of the spent brine containing additional toxic chemicals, remains a burning question [175].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There  are  also  some  sustainability  challenges  that  are  specific  to these Li recovery methods. In the precipitation technology, for example, a large volume of freshwater is needed as eluent and depending upon the geographical location, constant supply of such large volumes may not be feasible in the long term [24]. When it comes to solvent extraction, huge leftover volumes of organic solvent and ionic liquids used for extraction pose serious environmental concerns. Similarly, although the membrane-based methods possess the advantage of high Li yield, high energy consumption at the same time makes the process unsustainable on a larger scale. This is particularly true for the brines with low to medium Li concentration which is the case with most of the naturally occurring brines [19]. Even the ED-based membrane system is expected to consume significantly higher energy for brine containing Mg/Li ratio as low as 1 -10 [20]. Therefore, energy optimization requires serious attention for these advanced Li recovery methods.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Additionally,  the  excessive  Li  mining  from  the  natural  brine  has further led to water scarcity issues. For instance, scientists have warned that continued Li mining from Salar de Atacama in Chile (accounting for 25% of the global Li supply) will disrupt the water supply in the nearby areas [27,176,177]. Consequently, there has been a recent debate about considering  brine  as  'water ' or  'mineral ' due  to  the  water  scarcity experienced in these regions [178]. In this regard, the idea of utilizing RO desalination brine as a source of Li recovery can answer this concern as the Li source is being simultaneously used for producing water as well Li.  Meanwhile, implementing non-evaporative technologies for Li recovery will minimize the water loss in the aquifers as there is a possibility of reinjecting the Li deprived brines [24]. However, this might further complicate the situation by diluting the brine sources with Li deprived brine, which has to be addressed separately.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2. Cost
texts.text: 3.2. Cost

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Like all other systems, the cost of any Li recovery system is further divided into the 'capital ' and 'operational ' costs. In general, the capital cost of all the advanced Li recovery technologies is significantly high as compared  to the conventional evaporative methods  due  to the involvement of expensive materials and devices. Although the adsorption method has turned out to be highly efficient in terms of Li recovery and selectivity, even for the low Li brines, most of the advanced adsorbents are commercially unavailable while their synthesis is time and energy extensive. For example, 'Li sieves ' based on non-stochiometric lithium  manganese oxide has been reported as an efficient Li  adsorbent by several researchers. However, their synthesis is limited to small quantities at the lab scale while the large-scale usage is still economically infeasible. Moreover, due to the incomplete adsorption and presence  of  other  elements  in  significant  concentration,  more  advanced adsorbents  are  further  required  to  achieve  the  desired  purity  levels [179]. The scalability of the membrane-based methods is also limited due to the high equipment cost. Ion-exchange membranes as well as the complex design of the ED and the BPMED systems require significant initial  investment  which  is  usually  unjustified  based  on  the  current market demand and price of Li products. Moreover, membrane scaling, which leads to constant flux decline, is a general and a major issue in all the membrane-based Li recovery systems that incur significant operational costs during the process lifetime [180]. The pretreatment of brine, which is required to remove the particulate impurities before using it as a feed, also adds up to the operational cost which can be significant depending upon the brine source [181]. In the case of electrochemical methods, low Li uptake by the active electrode materials (~1 mol of Li by  150  g  of  active  material)  drastically  increases  the  material  cost limiting the scale-up of this technology for Li recovery [24]. A detailed techno-economic analysis by Albo et al. [7] concluded that most modern Li  recovery  methods  are  currently  economically  infeasible  for  largescale implementation. The operational cost of the modern Li recovery methods has been estimated to be significantly higher than the conventional evaporative techniques, especially when it comes to seawater brine due to its low Li concentration. The production cost of Li from seawater is estimated to be USD 80 per kg of product, as compared to only USD 2 when salt lake brine is used as feed [182]. NF has shown promising results of nearly USD 5 to 7 which is still significantly high [183]. A much lower operational cost of USD 2.18 was obtained in the case of MDC; however, the high capital cost and long-term operational costs remain as hurdles [184]. The best result so far was obtained in the case of selective ED i.e. USD 1.3; however, the capital investment and the pretreatment steps are expected to increase this value by several folds [185].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.3. Technology
texts.text: 3.3. Technology

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several modern technologies for Li recovery have been escalated to the  pilot  and  the  commercial  scale  to  recover  Li  from  salt  lake  and geothermal brines and China has been particularly instrumental in this regard [82]. This is because of the high Li concentration in these brines which makes the application of these methods greatly practical. However, when it comes to the Li recovery from seawater brine, which holds most of the global Li reserves, the technology readiness level (TRL) of these advanced methods has been estimated as 3 on the scale of 1 -9 [7]. This  is  primarily  because  of  the  extremely  low  Li  concentration  in seawater making these methods cost and energy-intensive. Therefore, most of these methods are still limited to lab-scale demonstration and require  a  great  deal  of  research  to  raise  their  TRL.  From  a  broader perspective, high energy consumption is the main technological barrier towards the large-scale implementation and to tackle this, maximizing the use of renewable energy sources has been proposed as a viable option to power these methods for brine concentration [24]. For instance, instead of using solar irradiance for brine evaporation, this renewable energy source could be used to power electrochemical or membranebased  brine  concentration  systems,  especially  in  the  regions  where solar energy is abundant throughout the year [186].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are some aspects specific to each brine recovery technology that need significant attention before their large-scale implementation can be realized. As far as adsorption is concerned, their long-term stability and regenerability remain a practical challenge, especially when the  concentration  of  other  cations  is  significantly  high  [8].  Solvent extraction methods face the issue of a large footprint. Systems based on mixed settler and centrifuge have been proposed to reduce the overall footprint but they undergo severe corrosion and require longer times to achieve equilibrium [82]. The membrane and electrochemical systems are highly sensitive to the feed salinity, especially with respect to the energy consumption and, therefore, more generalized systems that can take this variability into account need to be designed [20].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From the extensive research so far, it is evident that the low concentration of Li in the seawater brine concentration is the main hurdle towards the sizeable implementation of new technologies. However, the seawater RO brine, which is several times more concentrated with Li, is an attractive candidate for high yield Li recovery through the use of novel technologies. On average, the daily global production of seawater RO brine is nearly 141.m 3 [9] which is discarded back to the oceans as waste. According to rough estimates, seawater RO brine can carry up to 0.3 mg/L of Li [8] which translates to about 42 tons of Li being discarded back to the oceans on daily basis. Given the current market price of Li metal as 34 USD/kg (increased 7 times during the year 2020 -2021)

texts.orig: [187], nearly 1.5 million USD are thrown back to the oceans as waste in the form of Li. These massive figures call for urgent implementation of modern technologies to efficiently recover Li and other useful minerals from the seawater RO brine. It is to be noted; however, that the seawater RO brine is concentrated with several other unwanted minerals at the same time which poses additional challenges for selective Li recovery. In order to tackle this, the current water desalination technologies need a holistic  modification,  focusing  mainly  on  the  pretreatment  methods. From  the  grass  root  level,  the  methods  and  procedures  should  be designed  and  optimized  to  target  both  the  fresh  water  and  Li  (and potentially  other  useful  elements  such  as  Co  and  U)  as  the  primary products of the desalination process, thereby implementing the 'wasteto-wealth ' concept in the desalination industry.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions and outlook
texts.text: 4. Conclusions and outlook

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  exponential  increase  in  the  price  and  demand  of  Li  as  the portable electronic and EV industries soar, requires immediate attention for  sustainable  Li  recovery  from  alternate  resources  to  sustain  the supply-demand balance in the future markets. Moreover, the massive reserves of Li in seawater and the increasing marine pollution caused by the disposal of the desalination brine are the major driving forces towards the potential implementation of novel Li recovery technologies. Conventional evaporative technologies for Li recovery which rely on salt lakes and geothermal brine will likely fail to fulfill the rising Li demands due to the long cycle times and geographical variability. While the novel technologies offer a definite advantage over the conventional methods in  terms  of  product  throughput  and  process  efficiency,  it  can  be concluded that employing hybrid systems hold the strongest potential in terms of commercial applicability. For example, although adsorptionbased systems offer simpler design, lower cost and better efficiency for brines  with  low  Li  concentration,  most  of  the  developed  adsorbents suffer from poor regenerability and low selectivity. To overcome these issues, combination with other Li recovery techniques such as 'electrochemical ' and 'membrane-based ' , is a viable option for higher yield and product  purity.  For  instance,  adsorbents  integrated  with  membrane facilitate easier regeneration rather than granulated or powdered forms due  to  the  ease  of  separation.  Additionally,  a  combined  adsorptionfiltration effect achieved in this hybrid configuration could potentially enhance the overall selectivity of the adsorbents. Similarly, there is a strong need to improve the pre-treatment methods to aid the concentration  as  well  as  the  post-treatment  steps  that  can  provide  a  major breakthrough  in  the  recovery  of  Li  from  brine  via  modern  methods. Presently, it makes more sense to utilize the desalination brine for Li recovery as it offers dual benefits of higher product yield and environmental protection. While all the studies presented in this review claim promising results for the extraction of Li, most of them presented results on simulated or synthetic solutions for which the results might not be reproducible  for  real  desalination  brines.  Moreover,  a  comprehensive techno- and socio-economic analysis are essential to raise the TRL of new Li recovery methods so that they can be industrially scaled. More precisely, future research should be focused on scaling up the lab-scale technologies to analyze the feasibility of these technologies on a larger scale.
